era_context: The year 1804 stands at the pivot between the French Revolutionary era and the Napoleonic Empire. Napoleon Bonaparte, having consolidated power as First Consul since the 1799 coup, transformed the French Republic into a hereditary empire. Revolutionary ideals were simultaneously codified in law and betrayed in practice. Across the Atlantic, Haiti completed the only successful large-scale slave revolution in history, while the young United States embarked on its first great continental exploration. In the Islamic world, the Wahhabi movement seized the holy cities of Arabia, and in West Africa the Fulani jihad reshaped the political landscape of the Sahel. Britain remained the dominant naval and commercial power, locked in a struggle with Napoleonic France that shaped global affairs.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Southeast Asia beyond major courts
Interior southern Africa beyond the Cape

events.title: Haitian Declaration of Independence
events.description: On 1 January 1804, Jean-Jacques Dessalines proclaimed the independence of Haiti at Gonaïves, making it the second independent nation in the Western Hemisphere and the first founded by formerly enslaved people. The declaration followed over a decade of revolution against French colonial rule, including the defeat of a massive expeditionary force sent by Napoleon under General Leclerc in 1802–1803. Dessalines chose the indigenous Taíno name 'Hayti' to symbolise a complete break with the colonial past. The independence was accompanied by mass reprisals against the remaining French settler population in early 1804, ordered by Dessalines. Haiti's existence posed a profound ideological challenge to every slaveholding society in the Americas and was met with diplomatic isolation, most critically by the United States and France.
key_figures: Toussaint Louverture (predecessor leader, died in French captivity 1803)

sources.name: Haitian Declaration of Independence (1 January 1804), original text
Laurent Dubois, Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution (2004)
C.L.R. James, The Black Jacobins (1938, rev. 1963)

events.certainty_note: The declaration survives in multiple contemporary copies and is exhaustively documented in both Haitian and French sources.

events.title: Promulgation of the Napoleonic Code (Code civil des Français)
events.description: On 21 March 1804, the Code civil des Français was formally enacted, replacing the patchwork of feudal, customary, and Roman law that had governed France. Drafted by a commission under Jean-Jacques-Régis de Cambacérès and actively shaped by Napoleon himself during council debates, the code established legal equality before the law, secularised civil status, protected private property, and enshrined freedom of contract. However, it also reinforced patriarchal authority within the family, severely curtailed the legal rights of married women, and re-established slavery in French colonies (already enacted in 1802). The Code became one of the most influential legal documents in history, eventually adopted or adapted in dozens of countries across Europe, Latin America, the Middle East, and East Asia.

sources.name: Code civil des Français (1804), official text
Jean Carbonnier, Droit civil (various editions)
Proceedings of the Conseil d'État during Code drafting sessions

events.certainty_note: The legislative record, drafting deliberations, and official text are fully preserved.

events.title: Execution of the Duc d'Enghien
events.description: On 21 March 1804, Louis Antoine de Bourbon, Duc d'Enghien, was executed by firing squad in the moat of the Château de Vincennes after a summary military trial. He had been kidnapped from neutral Baden on Napoleon's orders on suspicion of involvement in royalist conspiracies against the Consulate, particularly the Cadoudal plot. The evidence against him was thin, and the violation of Baden's sovereignty and the execution of a Bourbon prince shocked European courts and deepened the ideological rift between Napoleonic France and the old monarchies. The affair is often cited as a turning point that hardened European resistance to Napoleon. Talleyrand reportedly commented it was 'worse than a crime — it was a blunder.'
key_figures: Louis Antoine de Bourbon, Duc d'Enghien (executed prince)

sources.name: Trial records and military tribunal proceedings at Vincennes
Philip Dwyer, Napoleon: The Path to Power 1769–1799 and Citizen Emperor (2007, 2013)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in French state records, diplomatic correspondence, and extensive contemporary press coverage.

events.title: Lewis and Clark Expedition departs
events.description: On 14 May 1804, the Corps of Discovery under Meriwether Lewis and William Clark departed Camp Dubois near the confluence of the Missouri and Mississippi Rivers, beginning their journey up the Missouri toward the Pacific coast. Commissioned by President Thomas Jefferson following the Louisiana Purchase of 1803, the expedition aimed to find a practical route across the continent, establish sovereignty claims, document the natural history and geography of the interior, and make diplomatic contact with Native American nations. By the end of 1804, the expedition had reached the Mandan and Hidatsa villages in present-day North Dakota, where they wintered and recruited Sacagawea as interpreter. The expedition profoundly shaped American continental ambitions and produced invaluable scientific and ethnographic records.

sources.name: Journals of Lewis and Clark (original expedition journals)
Stephen Ambrose, Undaunted Courage (1996)
Gary Moulton (ed.), The Definitive Journals of Lewis and Clark (1983–2001)

events.certainty_note: Detailed daily journals kept by multiple expedition members survive in full.

events.title: Napoleon crowned Emperor of the French
events.description: On 2 December 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte was crowned Emperor of the French in a lavish ceremony at Notre-Dame de Paris, with Pope Pius VII present but Napoleon famously placing the crown on his own head. A plebiscite earlier in the year had ratified the transformation of the Consulate into a hereditary empire with an overwhelming (though heavily managed) majority. The coronation represented the formal end of the First French Republic and the consolidation of Napoleon's personal rule within a monarchical framework, while retaining revolutionary symbols and the language of popular sovereignty. The event further alienated republican idealists who had supported the Revolution, even as it provided domestic stability after years of upheaval.
key_figures: Jacques-Louis David (official painter of the coronation)

sources.name: Official proceedings of the coronation ceremony, Archives nationales
Jacques-Louis David, The Coronation of Napoleon (painting, 1805–1807)
Philip Dwyer, Citizen Emperor: Napoleon in Power (2013)

events.certainty_note: One of the most documented ceremonies in European history, with extensive official records, diplomatic correspondence, and press coverage.

events.title: First Serbian Uprising begins
events.description: In February 1804, the First Serbian Uprising erupted against the rogue Janissary commanders (dahije) who had seized control of the Sanjak of Smederevo and tyrannised the local Serbian population, culminating in the 'Slaughter of the Knezes' in late January. Karađorđe (Đorđe Petrović) was elected leader of the revolt at an assembly in Orašac on 14 February. Initially framed as a movement to restore legitimate Ottoman authority against the Janissary usurpers, the uprising gradually evolved into a full-scale national liberation war. Though ultimately suppressed by 1813, it established the foundations of modern Serbian statehood and is regarded as the beginning of the Serbian Revolution.

sources.name: Leopold von Ranke, The History of Servia (Die Serbische Revolution, 1829)
Contemporary Ottoman administrative records and Serbian oral tradition accounts compiled by Vuk Karadžić

events.certainty_note: Well-attested in both Ottoman and Serbian sources, though exact dates of early skirmishes vary slightly between accounts.

events.title: Dessalines crowned Emperor of Haiti
events.description: On 6 October 1804, Jean-Jacques Dessalines was crowned Emperor Jacques I of Haiti, modelling his assumption of imperial title on Napoleon's own transformation of the French Republic. The Haitian constitution of 20 May 1805 (drafted under his direction from late 1804) would declare all Haitian citizens 'Black' regardless of skin colour and permanently ban white land ownership. Dessalines's reign was marked by authoritarian governance and the consolidation of a plantation-based economic system using forced labour (caporalisme agraire). His rule was cut short by assassination in October 1806, after which Haiti split into a northern kingdom and a southern republic.
key_figures: Jean-Jacques Dessalines (Emperor Jacques I of Haiti)

sources.name: Haitian imperial proclamation and contemporary diplomatic correspondence
Laurent Dubois, Haiti: The Aftershocks of History (2012)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Haitian records and foreign diplomatic dispatches.

events.title: Burr–Hamilton duel
events.description: On 11 July 1804, Vice President Aaron Burr fatally shot former Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton in a duel at Weehawken, New Jersey. Hamilton died the following day. The duel culminated years of political and personal rivalry, intensified by Hamilton's opposition to Burr's candidacy for governor of New York. Hamilton's death removed one of the most influential figures in American political and financial life, the architect of the federal financial system and the Federalist Party's intellectual leader. Burr was indicted for murder in both New York and New Jersey but never tried, and his political career was effectively destroyed, leading to his later involvement in the mysterious 'Burr Conspiracy' of 1805–07.
key_figures: Aaron Burr (Vice President of the United States)
Alexander Hamilton (former Secretary of the Treasury)

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts (New-York Evening Post, others)
Ron Chernow, Alexander Hamilton (2004)
Correspondence of Hamilton and Burr leading to the duel

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through contemporary newspapers, eyewitness accounts, and personal correspondence of both principals.

events.title: Trevithick's steam locomotive runs at Penydarren
events.description: On 21 February 1804, Richard Trevithick's unnamed steam locomotive hauled ten tons of iron, seventy men, and five wagons along the Merthyr Tydfil tramroad from Penydarren ironworks to the Abercynon canal basin, covering approximately 9.75 miles. This was the first recorded journey by a steam-powered locomotive on rails, a milestone in the history of transport technology. The locomotive worked but proved too heavy for the cast-iron tramway plates, which cracked under its weight, preventing regular service. Nevertheless, the demonstration proved the principle of steam rail traction and directly influenced subsequent locomotive development by engineers including George Stephenson.
key_figures: Samuel Homfray (Penydarren ironworks owner, who wagered on the demonstration)

sources.name: Contemporary letters of Richard Trevithick and accounts by witnesses
Anthony Burton, Richard Trevithick: Giant of Steam (2000)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Trevithick's own correspondence and reported in contemporary industrial and local records.

events.title: Twelfth Amendment ratified
events.description: On 15 June 1804, the Twelfth Amendment to the United States Constitution was ratified, fundamentally changing the presidential election process. It required electors to cast separate ballots for President and Vice President, resolving the crisis that had emerged in the 1800 election when Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr received equal electoral votes despite being on the same ticket. The amendment formalised the emerging two-party system by making the president-vice president ticket an explicit unit. It remains the operative framework for American presidential elections.

sources.name: Congressional records and state ratification documents
Tadahisa Kuroda, The Origins of the Twelfth Amendment (1994)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional amendment with precise ratification records preserved in federal and state archives.

events.title: Wahhabis capture Medina
events.description: In 1804, forces of the First Saudi State under Emir Saud ibn Abd al-Aziz captured Medina, bringing the second-holiest city of Islam under Wahhabi-Saudi control. This followed the capture of Mecca in 1803. The Saudi-Wahhabi forces systematically dismantled shrines, tombs, and domed structures they considered idolatrous, including the destruction of the famous domed tombs in the Jannat al-Baqi cemetery. The fall of the holy cities profoundly shocked the broader Islamic world and eventually prompted the Ottoman Empire to commission Muhammad Ali of Egypt to reconquer the Hejaz, launching the Ottoman-Saudi War of 1811–18.
key_figures: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab (Wahhabi movement founder, d. 1792, ideological basis)

sources.name: Ibn Bishr, Unwan al-Majd fi Tarikh Najd (19th-century Najdi chronicle)
Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia (2000)
Contemporary Ottoman diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Documented in multiple Arabic chronicles and Ottoman records, though exact dates within 1804 vary between sources.

events.title: Usman dan Fodio launches the Fulani Jihad
events.description: In February 1804, the Islamic scholar and reformer Usman dan Fodio was proclaimed Amir al-Mu'minin (Commander of the Faithful) by his followers at Gudu after fleeing persecution by the Sultan of Gobir. This hijra and proclamation formally launched the Fulani Jihad, which would overthrow the existing Hausa city-states and establish the Sokoto Caliphate, the largest state in nineteenth-century sub-Saharan Africa. Dan Fodio's movement was driven by a combination of Islamic reformism, opposition to syncretic religious practices, grievances of Fulani pastoralists against Hausa rulers, and social discontent. The Sokoto Caliphate would persist until British conquest in 1903 and its legacy continues to shape the politics and society of northern Nigeria.
key_figures: Usman dan Fodio (Islamic scholar, founder of Sokoto Caliphate)

sources.name: Usman dan Fodio, Tanbih al-Ikhwan and other treatises written 1804–1812
Murray Last, The Sokoto Caliphate (1967)
Mervyn Hiskett, The Sword of Truth: The Life and Times of the Shehu Usuman dan Fodio (1973)

events.certainty_note: Dan Fodio's own extensive writings provide primary documentation. Some uncertainty about exact chronology of early 1804 events.

events.title: Spain declares war on Britain
events.description: On 12 December 1804, Spain formally declared war on Britain, following the British attack on a Spanish treasure fleet at the Battle of Cape Santa Maria on 5 October, in which the frigate Mercedes was sunk with heavy loss of life. Britain had acted preemptively against Spanish bullion shipments that would finance France's war effort, despite Spain's nominal neutrality. The declaration brought Spain's navy and colonial empire into the war on France's side, setting the stage for the combined Franco-Spanish fleet's destruction at Trafalgar in October 1805. Spain's entry into the war also destabilised its control over its American colonies, as British naval supremacy disrupted transatlantic communication.
key_figures: Commodore Graham Moore (British naval commander at Cape Santa Maria)

sources.name: British and Spanish diplomatic correspondence, 1804
Charles Esdaile, The Peninsular War: A New History (2003)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic and naval records fully document both the Cape Santa Maria incident and the declaration of war.

events.title: Death of Immanuel Kant
events.description: On 12 February 1804, Immanuel Kant died in Königsberg at age 79. Kant is widely regarded as one of the most influential philosophers in Western history, having revolutionised epistemology, metaphysics, and ethics through works including the Critique of Pure Reason (1781), the Critique of Practical Reason (1788), and the Critique of Judgment (1790). His death came at a time when German Idealism — partly inspired by and partly reacting against his critical philosophy — was in full flower through the work of Fichte, Schelling, and the young Hegel. His influence extended far beyond philosophy into science, theology, political theory, and aesthetics.

sources.name: Ehregott Andreas Christoph Wasianski, Immanuel Kant in seinen letzten Lebensjahren (1804)
Manfred Kuehn, Kant: A Biography (2001)

events.certainty_note: Documented by his personal attendant Wasianski and widely reported in contemporary German press.

events.title: British and Foreign Bible Society founded
events.description: On 7 March 1804, the British and Foreign Bible Society was founded in London with the aim of distributing the Bible in vernacular languages worldwide. Its immediate catalyst was the lack of Welsh-language Bibles, championed by Thomas Charles of Bala, but its founders conceived a global mission. The Society became one of the most significant institutions of the nineteenth-century Protestant missionary movement, eventually distributing scriptures in hundreds of languages and contributing to literacy and linguistic documentation in many parts of the world. It also served as a model for other voluntary associations and Bible societies internationally.

sources.name: Minutes of the founding meeting, British and Foreign Bible Society archives
Leslie Howsam, Cheap Bibles: Nineteenth-Century Publishing and the British and Foreign Bible Society (1991)

events.certainty_note: Institutional records and founding documents fully preserved.

events.title: Morphine isolated by Friedrich Sertürner
events.description: Around 1804, the German pharmacist Friedrich Sertürner isolated the active alkaloid compound from opium, which he later named 'morphium' (morphine) after Morpheus, the Greek god of dreams. This was the first isolation of an active ingredient from a medicinal plant, marking the beginning of alkaloid chemistry and modern pharmacology. Sertürner's initial findings were published in 1805 in the Journal der Pharmacie, with a more complete account in 1817. The discovery transformed medicine by enabling precise dosing of pain relief, but also laid the groundwork for the modern opioid crisis. The exact date of isolation is uncertain, with work spanning 1803–1805.

sources.name: Friedrich Sertürner, publications in Journal der Pharmacie (1805) and Annalen der Physik (1817)
Andreas-Holger Maehle, 'The search for the active agent of opium' in Drugs on Trial (1999)

events.certainty_note: The isolation work spanned 1803–1805. The year 1804 is conventional; Sertürner's first publication appeared in 1805.

events.title: Ranjit Singh captures Amritsar
events.description: In 1804, Maharaja Ranjit Singh consolidated control over Amritsar, the holiest city of Sikhism and home to the Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple). This was a critical step in his construction of the Sikh Empire, which would eventually encompass the Punjab, Kashmir, and parts of the North-West Frontier. Control of Amritsar gave Ranjit Singh both religious legitimacy and a major commercial centre. His reign represented the high point of Sikh political power and the creation of the last independent Indian kingdom before British annexation in 1849.
key_figures: Ranjit Singh (Maharaja of the Sikh Empire)

sources.name: Sohan Lal Suri, Umdat-ut-Tawarikh (19th-century court chronicle)
J.S. Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1990)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Sikh court chronicles and British East India Company records of the period.

events.title: Hobart founded as British penal settlement in Van Diemen's Land
events.description: In early 1804, Lieutenant-Governor David Collins established a British settlement at Sullivan's Cove on the Derwent River, which became the town of Hobart. This followed a failed attempt at settlement at Sullivan Bay in Port Phillip (present-day Victoria) in 1803. The settlement was intended as a penal colony and a strategic move to prevent French claims to the island, following the French scientific expeditions under Baudin. Hobart became the second permanent European settlement in Australia after Sydney, and its establishment marked the beginning of the devastating dispossession of the Muwinina and other Palawa (Tasmanian Aboriginal) peoples.
key_figures: John Bowen (earlier settlement at Risdon Cove, 1803)

sources.name: David Collins, An Account of the English Colony in New South Wales (1798–1802) and colonial dispatches
James Boyce, Van Diemen's Land (2008)

events.certainty_note: Colonial administrative records and dispatches provide precise documentation.

events.title: Jiaqing Emperor suppresses the White Lotus Rebellion aftermath
events.description: By 1804, the Qing government under the Jiaqing Emperor was managing the aftermath of the White Lotus Rebellion (1796–1804), which had been largely suppressed by 1804 though scattered resistance continued. The rebellion, rooted in millenarian Buddhist sectarianism and peasant grievances, had devastated large parts of central China (Hubei, Sichuan, Shaanxi) and cost the Qing treasury an estimated 200 million taels of silver. The Jiaqing Emperor, who had taken personal control after the forced retirement of the corrupt minister Heshen in 1799, pursued anti-corruption campaigns in the military and civil bureaucracy. The rebellion exposed deep structural weaknesses in the Qing military and fiscal system that would intensify throughout the nineteenth century.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Susan Naquin, Millenarian Rebellion in China: The Eight Trigrams Uprising of 1813 (1976), with context on White Lotus

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Qing official records, though the exact end date of the rebellion is debated (1804 or late 1803).

events.title: Napoleon's invasion army assembles at Boulogne
events.description: Throughout 1804, Napoleon concentrated the Grande Armée — ultimately over 150,000 troops — at camps along the Channel coast near Boulogne-sur-Mer in preparation for a planned invasion of England. An elaborate flotilla of flat-bottomed boats was constructed to ferry the army across the English Channel. The invasion required temporary naval superiority in the Channel, which Napoleon never achieved due to the Royal Navy's blockade and the failure of Franco-Spanish fleet coordination. The camp at Boulogne served a dual purpose: it trained what became the most formidable army in Europe and applied constant strategic pressure on Britain. The army would be redirected eastward in 1805 for the Austerlitz campaign.
key_figures: Admiral Latouche-Tréville (French naval commander, d. August 1804)

sources.name: Napoleon's correspondence (Correspondance de Napoléon Ier)
David Chandler, The Campaigns of Napoleon (1966)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in French military records, diplomatic correspondence, and British intelligence reports.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1804 merit scrutiny. (1) Napoleon placing the crown on his own head: while this narrative is iconic and depicted in David's painting, some historians note that the ceremony was carefully negotiated with the Pope in advance, and Napoleon's self-crowning was an agreed part of the protocol rather than a spontaneous act of defiance — the dramatic framing is partly a product of later mythmaking. (2) The Haitian Declaration of Independence: the original document was long considered lost, and a copy was only rediscovered in the British National Archives in 2010 by Julia Gaffield. Some details of the declaration ceremony, including the extent of popular participation, rely on later accounts that may romanticise the event. (3) The 1804 massacre of French settlers in Haiti, ordered by Dessalines, is sometimes downplayed in sympathetic accounts of the revolution or exaggerated in hostile ones; the scale (estimates range from 3,000 to 5,000 killed) and the degree to which it was systematic versus chaotic remain debated. (4) The attribution of the first steam locomotive run to Trevithick on 21 February 1804 is generally accepted, but some sources place the successful run on a slightly different date, and there is debate about whether the locomotive completed the full distance or broke down partway.
historiographic_note: The year 1804 is among the best-documented in pre-modern history, with rich archives from European powers, the early American republic, and the Ottoman Empire. The principal biases are Eurocentric: the Napoleonic narrative dominates, sometimes eclipsing equally significant events in Haiti, West Africa, and Arabia. Haitian sources are notably sparse compared to French ones, creating an imbalance in the historical record of the revolution. The Fulani Jihad is documented primarily through Arabic-language texts by dan Fodio himself and later Sokoto court chroniclers, supplemented by modern historiography that began largely in the colonial era and carries its own biases. East Asian coverage relies heavily on Qing official records (Shilu), which are comprehensive but reflect court perspectives and may minimise the scale of rural unrest. Coverage of indigenous peoples — whether in North America, Australia, or elsewhere — is largely filtered through settler colonial sources.

graph_edges.note: Haitian independence led directly to Dessalines declaring himself Emperor later the same year
The Napoleonic Code and the imperial coronation were both elements of Napoleon's consolidation of personal power and institutional reform
The Enghien execution and elimination of royalist conspiracies cleared the path toward Napoleon's assumption of the imperial title
The Lewis and Clark expedition was a direct consequence of the Louisiana Purchase
Napoleon's imperial coronation helped trigger the formation of the Third Coalition against France
The First Serbian Uprising was the precursor to the second uprising and eventual Serbian autonomy
The Wahhabi capture of the holy cities provoked the Ottoman-Egyptian reconquest campaign
The Fulani Jihad launched in 1804 led to the fall of Hausa states and establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate
Spain's entry into the war placed its navy alongside France's, leading to the combined fleet's destruction at Trafalgar
Trevithick's locomotive demonstration proved steam rail traction and influenced Stephenson's later developments
The Grande Armée trained at Boulogne was redirected east for the Austerlitz campaign in 1805
